title: - - VECODER VARIATIONAL EMBEDDINGS FOR COM MUNITY DETECTION AND NODE REPRESENTATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - - VECODER VARIATIONAL EMBEDDINGS FOR COM MUNITY DETECTION AND NODE REPRESENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we study how to simultaneously learn two highly correlated tasks of graph analysis, i.e., community detection and node representation learning. We propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for jointly learning **V** ariational **E** mbeddings for **Co** mmunity **De** tection and node **R** epresentation. VECODER assumes that every node can be a member of one or more communities. The node embeddings are learned in such a way that connected nodes are not only “closer” to each other but also share similar community assignments. A joint learning framework leverages community-aware node embeddings for better community detection. We demonstrate on several graph datasets that VECODER effectively outperforms many competitive baselines on all three tasks i.e. node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection. We also show that VECODER is computationally efficient and has quite robust performance with varying hyperparameters.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graphs are flexible data structures that model complex relationships among entities, i.e. data points as nodes and the relations between nodes via edges. One important task in graph analysis is community detection, where the objective is to cluster nodes into multiple groups (communities). Each community is a set of densely connected nodes. The communities can be overlapping or non-overlapping, depending on whether they share some nodes or not. Several algorithmic (Ahn et al., 2010; Der´enyi et al., 2005) and probabilistic approaches (Gopalan & Blei, 2013; Leskovec & Mcauley, 2012; Wang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2013) to community detection have been proposed. Another fundamental task in graph analysis is learning the node embeddings. These embeddings can then be used for downstream tasks like graph visualization (Tang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2017) and classification (Cao et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2015).

In the literature, these tasks are usually treated separately. Although the standard graph embedding methods capture the basic connectivity, the learning of the node embeddings is independent of community detection. For instance, a simple approach can be to get the node embeddings via DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014) and get community assignments for each node by using k-means or Gaussian mixture model. Looking from the other perspective, methods like Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)), that focus on finding the community structure in the dataset, perform poorly for node-representation tasks e.g. node classification. This motivates us to study the approaches that jointly learn community-aware node embeddings.

Recently several approaches, like CNRL (Tu et al., 2018), ComE (Cavallari et al., 2017), vGraph (Sun et al. (2019)) etc, have been proposed to learn the node embeddings and detect communities simultaneously in a unified framework. Several studies have shown that community detection is improved by incorporating the node representation in the learning process (Cao et al., 2015; Kozdoba & Mannor, 2015). The intuition is that the global structure of graphs learned during community detection can provide useful context for node embeddings and vice versa.

The joint learning methods (CNRL, ComE and vGraph) learn two embeddings for each node. One node embedding is used for the node representation task. The second node embedding is the “context” embedding of the node which aids in community detection. As CNRL and ComE are based on Skip-Gram (Mikolov et al., 2013) and DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), they inherit “context” embedding from it for learning the neighbourhood information of the node. vGraph also requires two

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

node embeddings for parameterizing two different distributions. In contrast, we propose learning a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks. In this way, we not only get rid of an extraneous node embedding but also reduce the computational cost.

In this paper, we propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for jointly learning both community detection and node representation. The underlying intuition behind VECODER is that every node can be a member of one or more communities. However, the node embeddings should be learned in such a way that connected nodes are “closer” to each other than unconnected nodes. Moreover, connected nodes should have similar community assignments. Formally, we assume that for _i_ -th node, the node embeddings _**z** i_ are generated from a prior distribution _p_ ( _**z**_ ). Given _**z** i_ , the community assignments _ci_ are sampled from _p_ ( _ci|_ _**z** i_ ), which is parameterized by node and community embeddings. In order to generate an edge ( _i, j_ ), we sample another node embedding _**z** j_ from _p_ ( _**z**_ ) and respective community assignment _cj_ from _p_ ( _cj|_ _**z** j_ ). Afterwards, the node embeddings and the respective community assignments of node pairs are fed to a decoder. The decoder ensures that embeddings of both the nodes and the communities of connected nodes share high similarity. This enables learning such node embeddings that are useful for both community detection and node representation tasks.

We validate the effectiveness of our approach on several real-world graph datasets. In Sec. 4, we show empirically that VECODER is able to outperform the baseline methods including the direct competitors on all three tasks i.e. node classification, overlapping community detection and nonoverlapping community detection. Furthermore, we compare the computational cost of training different algorithms. VECODER is up to 40x more time-efficient than its competitors. We also conduct hyperparameter sensitivity analysis which demonstrates the robustness of our approach. Our main contributions are summarized below:
- We propose an efficient generative model called **VECODER** for joint community detection and node representation learning.
- We adopt a novel approach and argue that a single node embedding is sufficient for learning both the representation of the node itself and its context.

- Training VECODER is extremely time-efficient in comparison to its competitors.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Community Detection.** Early community detection algorithms are inspired from clustering algorithms (Xie et al., 2013). For instance, spectral clustering (Tang & Liu, 2011) is applied to the graph Laplacian matrix for extracting the communities. Similarly, several matrix factorization based methods have been proposed to tackle the community detection problem. For example, Bigclam (Yang & Leskovec (2013)) treats the problem as a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) task. It aims to recover the node-community affiliation matrix and learns the latent factors which represent community affiliations of nodes. Another method CESNA(Yang et al. (2013)) extends Bigclam by modelling the interaction between the network structure and the node attributes. The performance of matrix factorization methods is limited due to the capacity of the bi-linear models. Some generative models, like vGraph (Sun et al., 2019), Circles (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012)) etc, have also been proposed to detect communities in a graph.

**Node Representation Learning.** Many successful algorithms which learn node representation in an unsupervised way are based on random walk objectives (Perozzi et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2015; Grover & Leskovec, 2016; Hamilton et al., 2017). Some known issues with random-walk based methods (e.g. DeepWalk, node2vec etc) are: (1) They sacrifice the structural information of the graph by putting over-emphasis on the proximity information (Ribeiro et al., 2017) and (2) great dependence of the performance on hyperparameters (walk-length, number of hops etc) (Perozzi et al., 2014; Grover & Leskovec, 2016). Recently, Gilmer et al. (2017) recently showed that graph convolutions encoder models greatly reduce the need for using the random-walk based training objectives. This is because the graph convolutions enforce that the neighboring nodes have similar representations. Some interesting GCN based approaches include graph autoencoders e.g. GAE and VGAE(Kipf & Welling (2016b)) and DGI(Velickovic et al., 2019).
**Joint community detection and node representation learning.** In the literature, several attempts have been made to tackle both these tasks in a single framework. Most of these methods propose

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

an alternate optimization process, i.e. learn node embeddings and improve community assignments with them and vice versa (Cavallari et al., 2017; Tu et al., 2018). Some approaches, like CNRL (Tu et al., 2018) and ComE (Cavallari et al., 2017), are inspired from random walk, thus inheriting the shortcomings of random walk. Others, like GEMSEC (Rozemberczki et al. (2019), are limited to the detection of non-overlapping communities. There also exist some generative models like CommunityGAN (Jia et al. (2019)) and vGraph (Sun et al. (2019)) that jointly learn community assignments and node embeddings. Some methods have high computational complexity, i.e. quadratic to the number of nodes in a graph, e.g. M-NMF (Wang et al. (2017)) and DNR (Yang et al., 2016a). CNRL, ComE and vGraph require learning two embeddings for each node for simultaneously tackling the two tasks. Unlike them, VECODER learns a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks.

It is pertinent to highlight that although both vGraph and VECODER adopt a variational approach but the underlying models are quite different. vGraph assumes that each node can be represented as a mixture of multiple communities and is described by a multinomial distribution over communities, whereas VECODER models the node embedding by a single distribution. For a given node, vGraph, first draws a community assignment and then a connected neighbor node is generated based on the assignment. Whereas, VECODER draws the node embedding from prior distribution and then community assignment is conditioned on a single node only. In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECODER does not require it. VECODER relies on the decoder to ensure that embeddings of the connected nodes and their communities share high similarity with each other.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

## 3.1 PROBLEM FORMULATION

Suppose an undirected graph _G_ = ( _V, E_ ) with the adjacency matrix _**A** ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ and a matrix _**X** ∈_ R _[N][×][F]_ of _F_ -dimensional node features, _N_ being the number of nodes. Given _K_ as the number of communities, we aim to jointly learn the node embeddings and the community embeddings following a variational approach such that: (1) One or more communities can be assigned to every node and (2) the node embeddings can be used for both community detection and node classification.

## 3.2 VARIATIONAL MODEL

**Generative Model:** Let us denote the latent node embedding and community assignment for _i_ -th node by the random variables _**z** i ∈_ R _[d]_ and _ci_ respectively. The generative model is given by:

where _**c**_ = [ _c_ 1 _, c_ 2 _, · · · , cN_ ] and the matrix _**Z**_ = [ _**z**_ 1 _,_ _**z**_ 2 _, · · · ,_ _**z** N_ ] stacks the node embeddings. The joint distribution in (1) is mathematically expressed as

where _θ_ denotes the model parameters. Let us denote elements of _**A**_ by _aij_ . Following existing approaches (Kipf & Welling, 2016b; Khan et al., 2020), we consider _**z** i_ to be _i.i.d_ random variables. Furthermore, assuming _ci|_ _**z** i_ to be _i.i.d_ random variables, the joint distributions in (2) can be factorized as
where Eq. (5) assumes that the _edge decoder pθ_ ( _aij|ci, cj,_ _**z** i,_ _**z** j_ ) depends only on _ci, cj,_ _**z** i_ and _**z** j_ .

**Inference Model:** We aim to learn the model parameters _θ_ such that log( _pθ_ ( _**A**_ )) is maximized. In order to ensure computational tractability, we introduce the approximate posterior
where _I_ = ( _**A** ,_ _**X**_ ) if node features are available, otherwise _I_ = _**A**_ . We maximize the corresponding ELBO bound (for derivation, refer to the supplementary material), given by

where _DKL_ ( _.||._ ) represents the KL-divergence between two distributions. The distribution _qφ_ ( _**z** i,_ _**z** j, ci, cj|I_ ) in the third term of Eq. (7) is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions i.e.

## 3.3 DESIGN CHOICES

In Eq. (3), _p_ ( _**z** i_ ) is chosen to be the standard gaussian distribution for all _i_ . The corresponding approximate posterior _qφ_ ( _**z** i|I_ ) in Eq. (6), used as node embeddings encoder, is given by

The parameters of _qφ_ ( _**z** i|I_ ) can be learnt by any encoder network e.g. graph convolutional network (Kipf & Welling (2016a)), graph attention network ( Veliˇckovi´c et al. (2017)), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al. (2017)) or even two matrices to learn _**µ** i_ ( _I_ ) and diag( _**σ**_[2] _i_ ( _I_ )). Samples are then generated using reparametrization trick (Doersch (2016)).

For parameterizing _pθ_ ( _ci|_ _**z** i_ ) in Eq. (4), we introduce community embeddings _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, · · · ,_ _**g** K}_ ; _**g** k ∈_ R _[d]_ . The distribution _pθ_ ( _ci|_ _**z** i_ ) is then modelled as the softmax of dot products of _**z** i_ with _**g** k_ , i.e.

The corresponding approximate posterior _qφ_ ( _ci_ = _k|_ _**z** i, I_ ) in Eq. (6) is affected by the node embedding _**z** i_ as well as the neighborhood. To design this, our intuition is to consider the similarity of _**g** k_ with the embedding _**z** i_ as well as with the embeddings of the neighbors of the _i_ -th node. The overall similarity with neighbors is mathematically formulated as the average of the dot products of their embeddings. Afterwards a hyperparameter _α_ is introduced to control the bias between the effect of _**z** i_ and the set _Ni_ of the neighbors of the _i_ -th node. Finally, a softmax is applied as follows

Hence, Eq. (11) ensures that graph structure information is employed to learn community assignments instead of relying on an extraneous node embedding as done in (Sun et al., 2019; Cavallari et al., 2017). Finally, the choice of edge decoder in Eq. (5) is motivated by the intuition that the nodes connected by edges have a high probability of belonging to the same community and vice versa. Therefore we model the edge decoder as:

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

For better reconstructing the edges, Eq. (12) makes use of the community embeddings, node embeddings and community assignment information simultaneously. This helps in learning better node representations by leveraging the global information about the graph structure via community detection. On the other hand, this also forces the community assignment information to exploit the local graph structure via node embeddings and edge information.

## 3.4 PRACTICAL ASPECTS

The third term in Eq. (7) is estimated in practice using the samples generated by the approximate posterior. This term is equivalent to the negative of binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss between observed edges and reconstructed edges. Since community assignment follows a categorical distribution, we use Gumbel-softmax (Jang et al. (2016)) for backpropagation of the gradients. As for the second term of Eq. (7), it is also enough to set _M_ = 1, i.e. use only one sample per input node.

For inference, non-overlapping community assignment can be obtained for _i_ -th node as

To get overlapping community assignments for _i_ -th node, we can threshold its weighted probability vector at _ϵ_ , a hyperparameter, as follows

## 3.5 COMPLEXITY

Computation of dot products for all combinations of node and community embeddings takes _O_ ( _NKd_ ) time. Solving Eq. (11) further requires calculation of mean of dot products over the neighborhood for every node, which takes _O_ ( _|E|K_ ) computations overall as we traverse every edge for every community. Finally, we need softmax over all communities for every node in Eq. (10) and Eq. (11) which takes _O_ ( _NK_ ) time. Eq. (12) takes _O_ ( _|E|_ ) time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products. As a result, the overall complexity becomes _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ). This complexity is quite low compared to other algorithms designed to achieve similar goals (Cavallari et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016a).

We have selected 18 different datasets ranging from 270 to 126,842 edges. For nonoverlapping community detection and node classification, we use 5 the citation datasets (Bojchevski & G¨unnemann (2017); Yang et al. (2016b)). The remaining datasets (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012); Yang & Leskovec (2015)), used for overlapping community detection, are taken from SNAP repository (Leskovec & Krevl (2014)). Following (Sun et al., 2019), we take 5 biggest ground truth communities for youtube, amazon and dblp. Moreover, we also analyse the case of large number of communities. For this purpose, we prepare two subsets
Table 1: Every dataset has _|V|_ nodes, _|E|_ edges, _K_ communities and _|F |_ features. _|F |_ = N/A means that either the features were missing or not used.
of amazon dataset by randomly selecting 500 and 1000 communities from 2000 smallest communities in the amazon dataset.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 4.2 BASELINES

For overlapping community detection, we compare with the following competitive baselines: **MNMF** (Wang et al., 2017) learns community membership distribution by using joint non-negative matrix factorization with modularity based regularization. **BIGCLAM** (Yang & Leskovec (2013)) also formulates community detection as a non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) task. It simultaneously optimizes the model likelihood of observed links and learns the latent factors which represent community affiliations of nodes. **CESNA** (Yang et al. (2013)) extends BIGCLAM by statistically modelling the interaction between the network structure and the node attributes. **Circles** (Leskovec & Mcauley (2012)) introduces a generative model for community detection in ego-networks by learning node similarity metrics for every community. **SVI** (Gopalan & Blei (2013)) formulates membership of nodes in multiple communities by a Bayesian model of networks. **vGraph** (Sun et al. (2019)) simultaneously learns node embeddings and community assignments by modelling the nodes as being generated from a mixture of communities. **vGraph+** , a variant further incorporates regularization to weigh local connectivity. **ComE** (Cavallari et al. (2017)) jointly learns community and node embeddings by using gaussian mixture model formulation. **CNRL** (Tu et al., 2018) enhances the random walk sequences (generated by DeepWalk, node2vec etc) to jointly learn community and node embeddings. **CommunityGAN** (ComGAN)is a generative adversarial model for learning node embeddings such that the entries of the embedding vector of each node refer to the membership strength of the node to different communities. Lastly, we compare the results with the communities obtained by applying k-means to the learned embeddings of **DGI** (Velickovic et al., 2019).

For non-overlapping community detection and node classification, in addition to MNMF, DGI, CNRL, CommunityGAN, vGraph and ComE, we compare VECODER with the following baselines: **DeepWalk** (Perozzi et al. (2014)) makes use of SkipGram (Mikolov et al. (2013)) and truncated random walks on network to learn node embeddings. **LINE** (Tang et al. (2015)) learns node embeddings while attempting to preserve first and second order proximities of nodes. **Node2Vec** (Grover & Leskovec (2016)) learns the embeddings using biased random walk while aiming to preserve network neighborhoods of nodes. **Graph Autoencoder (GAE)** Kipf & Welling (2016b) extends the idea of autoencoders to graph datasets. We also include its variational counterpart i.e. **VGAE** . **GEMSEC** is a sequence sampling-based learning model which aims to jointly learn the node embeddings and clustering assignments.

## 4.3 SETTINGS

**For overlapping community detection** , we learn mean and log-variance matrices of 16dimensional node embeddings. We set _α_ = 0 _._ 9 and _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 3 in all our experiments. Following Kipf & Welling (2016b), we first pre-train a variational graph autoencoder. We perform gradient descent with Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba (2014)) and learning rate = 0 _._ 01. Community assignments are obtained using Eq. (14). For the baselines, we employ the results reported by Sun et al. (2019). For evaluating the performance, we use _F1-score_ and _Jaccard similarity_ .

**For non-overlapping community detection** , since the default implementations of most the baselines use 128 dimensional embeddings, for we use _d_ = 128 for fair comparison. Eq. (13) is used for community assignments. For vGraph, we use the code provided by the authors. We employ _normalized mutual information (NMI)_ and _adjusted random index (ARI)_ as evaluation metrics.

**For node classification** , we follow the training split used in various previous works (Yang et al., 2016b; Kipf & Welling, 2016a; Velickovic et al., 2019), i.e. 20 nodes per class for training. We train logistic regression using LIBLINEAR (Fan et al. (2008)) solver as our classifier and report the evaluation results on rest of the nodes. For the algorithms that do not use node features, we train the classifier by appending the raw node features with the learnt embeddings. For evaluation, we use _F1-macro_ and _F1-micro_ scores.
All the reported results are the average over five runs. Further implementation details can be found in the code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/1d95bf8f-8ce3-4870-a454-07db463b419f.

## 4.4 DISCUSSION OF RESULTS

In the following, we discuss the results to gain some important insights into the problem.
Table 2: F1 scores for overlapping communities. Best and second best values are bold and blue respectively.
Table 3: Jaccard scores for overlapping communities. Best and second best values are bold and blue respectively.
Table 4: Non-overlapping community detection results. Best and second best values are bold and blue respectively.

Table 5: Node classification results. Best and second best values are bold and blue respectively.

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Tables 2 and 3 summarize the results of the performance comparison for the overlapping community detection task.

First, we note that our proposed method VECODER outperforms the competitive methods on all datasets in terms of Jaccard Similarity. VECODER also outperforms its competitors on 12 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score. It is the second best method on the 13th dataset ( _fb0_ ). These results demonstrate the capability of VECODER to learn multiple community assignments quite well and hence reinforces our intuition behind the design of Eq. (11).

Second, we observe that there is no consistent performing algorithm among the competitive methods. That is, excluding VECODER , the best performance is achieved by vGraph/vGraph+ on 5, ComGAN on 4 and ComE on 3 out of 13 datasets in terms of F1-score. A a similar trend can be seen in Jaccard Similarity. Third, it is noted that all the methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task of community detection and node representation learning jointly. This supports our claim that treating the two tasks jointly results in better performance.

Fourth, we observe that vGraph+ results are generally better than vGraph. This is because vGraph+ incorporates a regularization term in the loss function which is based on Jaccard coefficients of connected nodes as edge weights. However, it should be noted that this prepossessing step is computationally expensive for densely connected graphs.

Tab. 4 shows the results on non-overlapping community detection. First, we observe that MNMF, DeepWalk, LINE and Node2Vec provide a good baseline for the task. However, these methods are not able to achieve comparable performance on any dataset relative to the frameworks that treat the two tasks jointly. Second, VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitors in NMI and ARI metrics, except for _CiteSeer_ where it achieves second best ARI. Third, we observe that GCN based models i.e. GAE, VGAE and DGI show competitive performance. That is, they achieve second best performance in all the datasets except _CiteSeer_ . In particular, DGI achieves second best NMI results in 3 out of 5 datasets and 2 out of 5 datsets in terms of ARI. Nonetheless, DGI results are not very competitive in Tab. 2 and Tab. 3, showing that while DGI can be a good choice for learning node embeddings for attributed graphs with non-overlapping communities, it is not the best option for non-attributed graphs or overlapping communities.

The results for node classification are presented in Tab. 5. VECODER achieves best F1-micro and F1-macro scores on 4 out of 5 datasets. We also observe that GCN based models i.e. GAE, VGAE and DGI show competitive performance, following the trend in results of Tab. 4. Furthermore, we note that the node classification results of CommunityGan (ComGAN) are quite poor. We think a potential reason behind it is that the node embeddings are constrained to have same dimensions as the number of communities. Hence, different components of the learned node embeddings simply represent the membership strengths of nodes for different communities. The linear classifiers may find it difficult to separate such vectors.

## 4.5 HYPERPARAMETER SENSITIVITY

We study the dependence of VECODER on _ϵ_ and _α_ by evaluating on four datasets of different sizes: _fb698_ ( _N_ = 61), _fb1912_ ( _N_ = 747), _amazon1000_ (N=1540) and _youtube_ ( _N_ = 5346). We sweep for _ϵ_ = _{_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 2 _, · · · ,_ 0 _._ 9 _}_ . For demonstrating effect of _α_ , we fix _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 3 and sweep for _α_ = _{_ 0 _._ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 2 _, · · · ,_ 0 _._ 9 _}_ . The average results of five runs for _ϵ_ and _α_ are given in Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b respectively. Overall VECODER is quite robust to the change in the values of _ϵ_ and _α_ . In case of _ϵ_ , we see a general trend of decrease in performance when the threshold _ϵ_ is set quite high e.g. _ϵ >_ 0 _._ 7. This is because the datasets contain overlapping communities and a very high _ϵ_ will cause the algorithm to give only the most probable community assignment instead of potentially providing multiple communities per node. However, for a large part of sweep space, the results are almost consistent. When _ϵ_ is fixed and _α_ is changed, the results are mostly consistent except when _α_ is set to a low value. Eq. (11) shows that in such a case the node itself is almost neglected and VECODER tends to assign communities based upon neighborhood only, which may cause a decrease in the performance. This effect is most visible in _amazon1000_ dataset because it has only 1.54 points on average per community i.e. there is a good chance for neighbours of a point of being in different communities. Therefore, only depending upon the neighbors will most likely result in poor results.

Figure 2: Comparison of running times of different algorithms.

## 4.6 TRAINING TIME

Now we compare the training times of different algorithms in Fig. 2. As some of the baselines are more resource intensive than others, we select aws instance type g4dn.4xlarge for fair comparison of training times. For vGraph, we train for 1000 iterations and for VECODER for 1500 iterations. For all other algorithms we use the default parameters as used in section 4.3. We observe that the methods that simply output the node embeddings take relatively less time compared to the algorithms that jointly learn node representations and community assignments e.g VECODER , vGraph and CNRL. Among these algorithms VECODER is the most time efficient. It consistently trains in less time compared to its direct competitors. For instance, it is about 12 times faster than ComE for _CiteSeer-full_ and about 40 times faster compared to vGraph for _Cora-full_ dataset. This provides evidence for lower computational complexity of VECODER in Section 3.5.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We propose a scalable generative method VECODER to simultaneously perform community detection and node representation learning. Our novel approach learns a single community-aware node embedding for both the representation of the node and its context. VECODER is scalable due to its low complexity, i.e. _O_ ( _|E|K_ + _NKd_ ). The experiments on several graph datasets show that VECODER consistently outperforms all the competitive baselines on node classification, overlapping community detection and non-overlapping community detection tasks. Moreover, training the VECODER is highly time-efficient than its competitors.

- Sheng Gao, Ludovic Denoyer, and Patrick Gallinari. Temporal link prediction by integrating content and structure information. In _Proceedings of the 20th ACM international conference on Information and knowledge management_ , pp. 1169–1174, 2011.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting generative model (VECODER) for joint node representation and community detection, motivated by the efficiency gain of using a single embedding. The methodology is generally well-structured, and the empirical results across 18 datasets are extensive. However, there are significant technical concerns regarding the formulation of the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), specifically a mismatch between the joint distribution factorization and the resulting KL terms, as well as an underspecified edge decoder that complicates reproducibility. Some claims regarding the 'removed extraneous embedding' are slightly overstated since the community embeddings $g_k$ effectively satisfy a similar role to context embeddings in other models.

comments.title: Inconsistency in ELBO derivation and KL-divergence terms
comments.quote: \mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{A}| \dots)] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}_i | I) || p(\mathbf{z}_i)) - D_{KL}(q_\phi(c_i | \mathbf{z}_i, I) || p_\theta(c_i | \mathbf{z}_i))
comments.explanation: In Equation 7, the authors present the ELBO. Standard variational inference for a joint posterior $q(z, c)$ would require the KL term to be $D_{KL}(q(z, c) || p(z, c))$. Given the factorization $q(z, c) = q(z)q(c|z)$ and $p(z, c) = p(z)p(c|z)$, the KL term should indeed decompose into a sum. However, the third term in Eq 7 uses $p_\theta(c_i | \mathbf{z}_i)$, which is defined in Eq 10 as a learnable distribution parameterized by community embeddings $g_k$. If the prior $p(c|z)$ is itself a learnable parameter of the model, the ELBO derivation is non-standard. Usually, $p(c|z)$ would be a fixed prior (e.g., uniform). If $p_\theta(c_i | \mathbf{z}_i)$ is learnable, the KL term might vanish or behave unpredictably during optimization. Furthermore, Eq 7 sums over individual $i$, but the reconstruction term involves the whole adjacency matrix $\mathbf{A}$, leading to a summation mismatch in the formal expression.

comments.title: Underspecified Edge Decoder
comments.quote: p_\theta(a_{ij} | c_i, c_j, \mathbf{z}_i, \mathbf{z}_j) = \sigma(f(\mathbf{z}_i, \mathbf{z}_j) + g(c_i, c_j))
comments.explanation: In Equation 12, the authors define the edge decoder using abstract functions $f$ and $g$. While the text suggests these functions ensure 'high similarity,' the exact functional forms (e.g., dot product, Hadamard product, or a MLP) are never specified in the methodology. Given that this is the core of the generative process, the lack of a concrete definition for $f(\cdot)$ and $g(\cdot)$ makes the model impossible to reproduce exactly without inspecting the provided external code. Additionally, since $c_i$ is a categorical cluster index, the operation $g(c_i, c_j)$ likely refers to the similarity of community embeddings $\mathbf{g}_{c_i}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{c_j}$, but this is not explicitly stated.

comments.title: Conceptual ambiguity regarding 'single' vs 'double' embeddings
comments.quote: In contrast, we propose learning a single community-aware node representation which is directly used for both tasks. In this way, we not only get rid of an extraneous node embedding but also reduce the computational cost.
comments.explanation: The authors argue that they improve upon methods like vGraph or ComE by using only one node embedding. However, the model introduces community embeddings $\{\mathbf{g}_1, \dots, \mathbf{g}_K\}$ in Eq 10. In a community detection context, if $K$ is large (as in the Amazon1000 experiment), these community embeddings act very similarly to the 'context' or 'cluster' embeddings used in the baselines. The memory reduction claim is valid relative to node-specific context embeddings, but the distinction is less 'binary' than the prose suggests, especially since the community embeddings are used in the dot product similarly to the second embedding in Skip-gram based models.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency in Generative Process
comments.quote: In order to generate an edge (i, j), we sample another node embedding $\mathbf{z}_j$ from $p(\mathbf{z})$ and respective community assignment $c_j$ from $p(c_j|\mathbf{z}_j)$.
comments.explanation: The generative process described in Section 1 and 3.2 assumes nodes and их characteristics are generated independently, and then edges are conditioned on those characteristics. In Eq 12, $a_{ij}$ (the edge) is generated based on $c_i, c_j, z_i, z_j$. However, in standard $p(A|Z)$ formulations for variational autoencoders on graphs, the sampling of $i$ and $j$ from the node set is usually assumed to be uniform or follows the graph's degree distribution. The text says 'we sample another node embedding... to generate an edge,' which suggests a node-based sampling that doesn't clearly account for how the 'non-edges' are generated/modelled in the likelihood, especially given the use of BCE loss mentioned in Section 3.4.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to jointly learning node representations and community assignments via a variational generative model. The model design is clean, with a clear probabilistic interpretation and efficient inference. The empirical evaluation is extensive and shows strong performance across multiple tasks and datasets. The complexity analysis aligns with the reported runtime improvements, supporting the claim of efficiency. However, there are several technical issues in the mathematical formulation and notation consistency that need to be addressed, particularly regarding the factorization of the inference model and the definition of the edge decoder, which currently raise concerns about correctness and reproducibility.

comments.title: Incorrect factorization of joint posterior in inference model
comments.quote: The distribution _qφ_ ( _**z** i,_ _**z** j, ci, cj|I_ ) in the third term of Eq. (7) is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions i.e.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the joint approximate posterior over two nodes and their community assignments is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions, but it does not specify what those distributions are—there is no equation following this statement. Given the structure of the ELBO and typical practice in variational GNNs, one would expect a factorization like qφ(zi, ci|I)qφ(zj, cj|I) or similar. However, without an explicit equation, this is ambiguous. The omission breaks the logical flow and prevents a reader from verifying the correctness of the inference procedure. This is especially problematic because the ELBO term depends on this factorization, and an incorrect one could invalidate the variational bound. The lack of a defining equation here is not a forward reference issue, as the section ends without providing it.

comments.title: Missing equation for edge decoder probability
comments.quote: Finally, the choice of edge decoder in Eq. (5) is motivated by the intuition that the nodes connected by edges have a high probability of belonging to the same community and vice versa. Therefore we model the edge decoder as:
comments.explanation: The text promises a definition of the edge decoder pθ(aij|ci, cj, zi, zj) but provides no equation. This is a critical omission because the decoder determines how structure, embeddings, and communities interact to reconstruct the graph. Without this formula, the core generative mechanism remains unspecified. While one might infer it from context (e.g., a similarity function over zi, zj and community overlap), the absence of a concrete expression—especially given community assignments ci, cj—are discrete variables—makes it impossible to reproduce the model. Standard decoders (like inner product or GNN-based) would depend only on zi, zj, not ci, cj directly. The mention of community assignments in the decoder suggests a more complex design that must be defined.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of community assignment variable
comments.quote: Given _**z** i_ , the community assignments _ci_ are sampled from _p_ ( _ci|_ _**z** i_ ), which is parameterized by node and community embeddings.
comments.explanation: The notation ci is used to denote a single community assignment for node i, but later the model is applied to overlapping communities. If ci is a single community index, it cannot represent multiple memberships. However, in Section 3.4, the method obtains overlapping communities by thresholding a probability vector, implying that ci should be a vector or a distribution. The text never clarifies whether ci is scalar (hard assignment) or categorical (soft membership). This creates ambiguity: is the model learning a distribution over communities or multiple binary memberships? The use of Gumbel-softmax supports the latter, but without clarifying ci's domain, the generative process is underspecified.

comments.title: Unspecified form of community assignment posterior
comments.quote: The corresponding approximate posterior _qφ_ ( _ci_ = _k|_ _**z** i, I_ ) in Eq. (6) is affected by the node embedding _**z** i_ as well as the neighborhood... Finally, a softmax is applied as follows
comments.explanation: The paper describes an intuition for computing qφ(ci=k|zi, I) using both zi and neighbor embeddings but fails to provide the actual equation. While Eq. (11) suggests a form of aggregation, it does not appear in the text—only a description of its components. This missing equation undermines reproducibility. The neighborhood influence is described as an average of dot products, but it is unclear whether this is aggregated before or after the softmax, or how exactly α balances node and neighbor contributions. Given that this is a core innovation (using structure to inform community inference), the lack of a formal definition is a significant gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in edge decoder input dependency
comments.quote: Eq. (5) assumes that the _edge decoder pθ_ ( _aij|ci, cj,_ _**z** i,_ _**z** j_ ) depends only on _ci, cj,_ _**z** i_ and _**z** j_
comments.explanation: While this dependency seems reasonable, the paper does not explain how discrete community assignments ci, cj are used in a differentiable decoder. If ci is a one-hot or sampled category, using it directly in a neural decoder with zi and zj would require careful handling (e.g., through embeddings or gating). Without details, it's unclear whether the model uses the sampled ci or the full distribution over communities. This affects gradient estimation (e.g., via Gumbel-softmax). The claim that this helps 'learning better node representations' lacks mechanistic justification when the decoder's form is missing.

comments.title: Underspecified neighborhood aggregation in inference
comments.quote: The overall similarity with neighbors is mathematically formulated as the average of the dot products of their embeddings.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the neighborhood influence is based on the average of dot products of neighbor embeddings with community centers gk. However, it does not specify with what these dot products are computed—presumably with gk, but this is not stated. Moreover, it doesn’t define over which nodes or how the average is computed (e.g., uniform weight, attention, normalized by degree). The omission of these details—especially for a model claiming to leverage local structure for community inference—makes it difficult to assess the novelty or reproduce the method.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: VECODER presents an interesting approach to joint community detection and node representation learning by using a single embedding per node. The paper is well-structured and demonstrates strong empirical results across multiple tasks. However, there are some significant concerns regarding the mathematical formulation and claims made. The most serious issue is a contradiction between the claim that VECODER 'does not require edge information in the generative process' while the edge decoder clearly uses edge information. Additionally, the edge decoder formulation could benefit from better justification. Despite these issues, the empirical results are convincing and the computational efficiency claims appear well-supported.

comments.title: Contradiction about edge information usage
comments.quote: In simple terms, vGraph also needs edge information in the generative process whereas VECODER does not require it.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the model formulation. Equation (5) defines an edge decoder pθ(aij|ci, cj, zi, zj) that explicitly conditions on edge existence, and equation (12) further specifies this decoder using both node and community embeddings. The generative process clearly uses edge information to reconstruct the adjacency matrix. While VECODER may not use edges during the initial sampling of zi and ci, the overall model does require and use edge information through the decoder. This undermines a key claimed advantage over vGraph.

comments.title: Edge decoder formulation needs justification
comments.quote: pθ(aij = 1|ci, cj, zi, zj) = σ(zi^T zj + g_ci^T g_cj)
comments.explanation: The edge decoder combines node embeddings and community embeddings through a simple sum of inner products. While mathematically valid, this formulation lacks proper justification. Why use the inner product of community embeddings g_ci^T g_cj? This term encourages nodes in communities with similar embeddings to connect, but this modeling choice is not motivated theoretically. The authors should explain why this formulation is preferable to alternatives, such as using only node embeddings (like VGAE) or only community information.

comments.title: Inconsistent embedding dimensions across tasks
comments.quote: For overlapping community detection, we learn mean and log-variance matrices of 16-dimensional node embeddings... For non-overlapping community detection... we use d = 128 for fair comparison.
comments.explanation: The authors use different embedding dimensions for different tasks (16-dim for overlapping, 128-dim for non-overlapping) citing 'fair comparison' with baselines. However, this makes it unclear whether the performance difference is due to the model architecture or the embedding dimensionality. Since VECODER uses a single embedding for both tasks, it should use the same dimensionality across all experiments to demonstrate the true effectiveness of the approach.

comments.title: Underspecified encoder architecture
comments.quote: The parameters of qφ(zi|I) in Eq. (6), used as node embeddings encoder, is given by... The parameters of qφ(zi|I) can be learnt by any encoder network e.g. graph convolutional network...
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify which encoder architecture was actually used in the experiments. While flexibility is good, reproducibility requires knowing the specific implementation. The authors should state which encoder was used (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, etc.) along with its architecture details (number of layers, hidden dimensions, activation functions, etc.). This is particularly important given that the encoder significantly impacts the quality of the learned embeddings.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for conditional independence assumption
comments.quote: The distribution qφ(zi, zj, ci, cj|I) in the third term of Eq. (7) is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions
comments.explanation: The factorization in equation (8) assumes that (zi, ci) and (zj, cj) are conditionally independent given the observed information I. However, for adjacent nodes (i,j) ∈ E, this assumption may not hold as the embeddings and community assignments are likely correlated. The authors do not justify why this simplification is valid or discuss its potential impact on the model's ability to capture dependencies between connected nodes.

comments.title: Ambiguous computational efficiency claim
comments.quote: VECODER is up to 40x more time-efficient than its competitors.
comments.explanation: The 40x speedup claim is based on Figure 2, but the complexity analysis in section 3.5 does not clearly support such a dramatic improvement. The complexity O(|E|K + NKd) is linear in edges and nodes, which is good, but without comparing it to the actual complexities of baselines, the 40x claim seems exaggerated. The authors should provide complexity analysis for the baselines or temper this claim to better reflect the theoretical expectations.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents VECODER, a generative model for joint node representation and community detection. The approach is conceptually sound, and the empirical results demonstrate strong performance and efficiency. However, there are significant mathematical notation errors and inconsistencies in the definition of the probabilistic generative model in Section 3.2, which make the mathematical formulation difficult to follow.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in joint distribution
comments.explanation: The term for the edge decoder involves the product over all $(i,j) \in E$. However, $a_{ij}$ is typically defined for all pairs $(i,j)$, whereas $|E|$ only denotes existing edges. If $a_{ij}$ represents the connectivity (e.g., 0 or 1), the product over the adjacency matrix should ideally cover all pairs to account for both existing and missing edges, or the range of the product is missing a summation/integral over latent variables. Further, in eq. (2) and (5), the notation $a_{ij}$ is used where the text implies summation over all nodes, but the product only mentions edges.

comments.title: Undefined terms in ELBO derivative
comments.explanation: Equation (7) is not explicitly provided in the text (the paper jumps from the logic of (6) to the label (7) without displaying the ELBO equation). Additionally, the factorization for the third term is provided in (8), but the relationship between the joint of all nodes/communities and the pairwise expectations is not clear. It is technically underspecified how the global objective (7) maps to the pairwise loss (8) without providing the full KL derivation.

comments.title: Notation confusion in Eq(11)
comments.explanation: The index $k$ in the softmax refers to the community index. The expression inside the softmax is a scalar per community $k$, which is correct. However, $q_{\phi}$ is defined as a distribution over community assignments given node $i$. The logic is clear, but the notation $\text{softmax}_k$ acts on the argument vector indexed by $k$; this is a common shorthand, but technically ambiguous because the expression inside depends on $k$ and the softmax is applied over the discrete set of communities $K$. While contextually clear for a reader in the field, it is formally loose.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes VECODER, a variational generative model for joint community detection and node representation learning. The core idea of learning a single community-aware node embedding for both tasks is novel and well-motivated. The empirical evaluation is extensive, covering multiple datasets and baselines, and the reported results are strong, particularly in terms of computational efficiency. However, the paper contains several significant issues. The mathematical derivation of the ELBO in Eq. (7) contains a critical error in the sign of the KL divergence term, which undermines a core theoretical contribution. Furthermore, the description of the inference model and the treatment of the node features matrix `X` are ambiguous and potentially inconsistent, which could hinder reproducibility. The complexity analysis in Section 3.5 is also misleading, not accounting for the GCN encoder or the Gumbel-Softmax sampling. These issues are major and require careful correction.

comments.title: Sign error in ELBO derivation
comments.quote: where DKL(.||.) represents the KL-divergence between two distributions. The distribution qφ(zi, zj, ci, cj|I) in the third term of Eq. (7) is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions i.e.
comments.explanation: The ELBO in Eq. (7) is presented as: L = ... + DKL(qφ(ci|zi, I) || pθ(ci|zi)) + ... . The standard form of the ELBO includes a negative KL divergence term (i.e., -DKL(q||p)) to encourage the approximate posterior to stay close to the prior. The sign before the KL term in the equation is positive, which would encourage divergence, not convergence. This is a critical error in the variational inference formulation, as it would push the posterior away from the prior, likely leading to an incorrect optimization objective. I checked the context to see if this is a misprint, but the text explicitly states 'DKL(.||.) represents the KL-divergence' and the equation directly contradicts the standard variational bound.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of inference model input
comments.quote: Inference Model: We aim to learn the model parameters θ such that log(pθ(A)) is maximized. In order to ensure computational tractability, we introduce the approximate posterior where I = (A, X) if node features are available, otherwise I = A.
comments.explanation: The paper defines the inference model's conditioning variable as `I`, which is either the adjacency matrix `A` alone or the pair `(A, X)`. This is ambiguous. In line with the generative model described in Eq. (3)-(5), the model is conditioned on the graph `G`. The inference model is supposed to approximate the posterior over the latent variables `(Z, c)` given the graph. The introduction of `I` as a shorthand for `(A, X)` or just `A` is confusing. While the GCN encoder (mentioned in Section 3.3) naturally handles both, the notation `qφ(zi, zj, ci, cj|I)` is not standard. It is unclear whether the model is unconditionally generative or conditionally generative. A variational autoencoder for a graph should have the graph as the data point, not a conditioning variable in the same way as a conditional VAE. The authors seem to be using `I` to indicate the encoder's input, but the theoretical framing suggests a conditional distribution, which is not correctly set up.

comments.title: Incorrect complexity claim omits key components
comments.quote: Computation of dot products for all combinations of node and community embeddings takes O(NKd) time. Solving Eq. (11) further requires calculation of mean of dot products over the neighborhood for every node, which takes O(|E|K) computations overall as we traverse every edge for every community. Finally, we need softmax over all communities for every node in Eq. (10) and Eq. (11) which takes O(NK) time. Eq. (12) takes O(|E|) time for all edges as we have already calculated the dot products. As a result, the overall complexity becomes O(|E|K + NKd).
comments.explanation: The complexity analysis in Section 3.5 is misleading. It analyzes the per-iteration cost of the final loss computation but omits the most computationally expensive part: the forward pass of the encoder network (e.g., a GCN) to obtain the parameters of `qφ(zi|I)`. A typical GCN for encoding N nodes with d-dimensional embeddings has complexity O(|E| d^2) or O(|E| d) depending on the implementation. This is a dominant term that is not accounted for. Furthermore, the analysis also ignores the cost of the Gumbel-Softmax sampling for the categorical variables. While the final complexity might still be lower than competitors, the provided analysis is incomplete and therefore disingenuous.

comments.title: Inconsistency in handling node features for non-attributed datasets
comments.quote: I = (A, X) if node features are available, otherwise I = A. The parameters of qφ(zi|I) can be learnt by any encoder network e.g. graph convolutional network...
comments.explanation: The paper states that node features `X` are available for citation datasets but `N/A` for others (e.g., Facebook). For the overlapping community detection task (Tables 2 and 3), most datasets do not have features. The description of the encoder `qφ(zi|I)` says it can be a GCN. A standard GCN requires a feature matrix as input. If features are not available, the authors must specify how `I = A` is used as input to a GCN (e.g., using the identity matrix or one-hot encoding). This crucial implementation detail for reproducibility is missing. The paper is underspecified here; a reader cannot reproduce the results on non-attributed graphs.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the factorization of the ELBO's third term
comments.quote: The distribution qφ(zi, zj, ci, cj|I) in the third term of Eq. (7) is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions i.e.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the joint posterior is factorized into two conditionally independent distributions but does not provide the explicit factorization formula. Given the context, it likely means `q(zi, ci, zj, cj|I) = q(zi|I) q(ci|zi, I) q(zj|I) q(cj|zj, I)`, assuming independence between nodes `i` and `j`. However, this is a critical assumption for the variational approximation that is not explicitly stated. The text 'factorized into two conditionally independent distributions' is vague. This lack of specificity makes the derivation of the ELBO term (the reconstruction loss) unclear.

comments.title: Potential omission of negative log-likelihood in ELBO
comments.quote: This term is equivalent to the negative of binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss between observed edges and reconstructed edges.
comments.explanation: The ELBO is usually written as `log p(x) >= E_q(z|x)[log p(x|z)] - KL(q(z|x)||p(z))`. In Eq. (7), the third term is the log-likelihood (reconstruction term). The text later says it is equivalent to 'negative BCE'. This is correct if `log p(A|Z,c) = -BCE`. However, the ELBO derivation in the supplementary material (mentioned but not provided in the main text) would show this. The phrasing is slightly roundabout but functionally correct. It is not a serious error but could be clearer.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel VAE framework for jointly learning node representations and detecting communities, offering a computationally efficient alternative to existing joint models. Empirical results demonstrate competitive or superior performance on several benchmarks. However, the manuscript contains a critical index error in Equation (11) where the bias terms for the denominator summation do not align with the community being evaluated. Additionally, the text makes a factual claim in the results section that contradicts the provided tables, and the default hyperparameter $\alpha = 0.9$ lacks justification. These issues require correction for the paper to be accepted.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents VECODER, a unified variational framework for joint community detection and node representation learning. The motivation is clear, and the experimental results generally support the claim of competitive performance across multiple tasks. However, the manuscript contains several methodological and logical inconsistencies that require clarification before publication. Most notably, the evaluation of overlapping community detection relies on k-means, which inherently produces hard partitions, contradicting the task's definition. Additionally, key mathematical definitions for the community assignment and edge decoder are missing from the text, and there are unexplained discrepancies in embedding dimensions between tasks. The authors also make a factual claim regarding second-best performing baselines that does not hold for certain datasets. Addressing these issues will significantly strengthen the paper's reproducibility and rigor.

comments.title: Methodologically flawed baseline for overlapping community detection
comments.quote: Lastly, we compare the results with the communities obtained by applying k-means to the learned embeddings of DGI
comments.explanation: The authors list DGI+k-means as a baseline for *overlapping* community detection. K-means is a hard clustering algorithm that explicitly partitions nodes into disjoint clusters, making it fundamentally unsuitable for evaluating overlapping community detection. Using it here not only invalidates the comparison on overlapping metrics (F1/Jaccard) but also misrepresents the baseline's capabilities. The authors should either replace k-means with a proper soft/overlapping baseline (e.g., fuzzy c-means or direct probability thresholding) or remove this comparison from the overlapping task entirely.

comments.title: Missing critical equations for community assignment and edge decoder
comments.quote:
The distribution pθ (ci|zi) is then modelled as the softmax of dot products of zi with gk , i.e.

 The corresponding approximate posterior qφ (ci = k|zi, I) in Eq. (6) is affected by the node embedding zi as well as the neighborhood.

Afterwards a hyperparameter α is introduced to control the bias between the effect of zi and the set Ni of the neighbors of the i-th node. Finally, a softmax is applied as follows

 Hence, Eq. (11) ensures that graph structure information is employed...
comments.explanation: The text repeatedly references Eqs. (10), (11), and (12) but the actual mathematical formulations are omitted from the provided text (likely due to OCR extraction or formatting loss). Without the explicit expressions for the community assignment logits, the α-weighted neighbor aggregation, and the edge decoder's interaction terms, readers cannot reproduce the model. Given that the novelty heavily relies on how community embeddings and node embeddings are jointly conditioned, providing the exact equations is essential.

comments.title: Unjustified discrepancy in embedding dimensions across tasks
comments.quote: For overlapping community detection, we learn mean and log-variance matrices of 16dimensional node embeddings. We set α = 0.9 and ϵ = 0.3 in all our experiments. ... For non-overlapping community detection , since the default implementations of most the baselines use 128 dimensional embeddings, for we use d = 128 for fair comparison.
comments.explanation: The authors use a 16-dimensional embedding space for overlapping community detection but switch to 128 dimensions for non-overlapping detection and node classification. This 8x difference is never justified. While smaller dimensions may prevent overfitting on the small overlapping datasets, the lack of ablation or reasoning leaves it unclear whether VECODER's performance on overlapping tasks is a result of architectural design or simply an advantage from using a different dimensional space than the baselines (which presumably used their standard settings). This makes cross-task performance comparison and fair baseline matching difficult to interpret.

comments.title: Incorrect claim regarding second-best baseline capabilities
comments.quote: Third, it is noted that all the methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task of community detection and node representation learning jointly. This supports our claim that treating the two tasks jointly results in better performance.
comments.explanation: In Table 4 (non-overlapping community detection), DGI achieves the second-best NMI on CiteSeer (37.8%) and second-best ARI on CiteSeer-full (40.6%). However, DGI is a contrastive representation learning method that does not perform community detection natively; it requires a post-hoc clustering step (which the authors themselves note is k-means). Therefore, DGI is not a 'joint' community detection and representation model. The sweeping claim that 'all methods which achieve second best performance are solving the task... jointly' is factually inaccurate and weakens the argument that joint learning is strictly necessary for competitive results.
